speaker: patient
text: Hi, Doctor. I’ve been feeling really unwell since yesterday—especially this morning.

speaker: doctor
text: Thanks for coming in. Let’s start by checking your vitals. I’ll take your heart rate now.

speaker: doctor
text: Your heart rate is 85 beats per minute—within normal range for you.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve had nausea all day—it came on suddenly and hasn’t let up.

speaker: doctor
text: Based on your history, stress test results, and current presentation, I’m diagnosing Coronary Artery Disease as the primary condition today.

speaker: doctor
text: Your hemoglobin level is 135 g/L—solid, no anemia concerns right now.

speaker: doctor
text: Your temperature is elevated at 39.2°C—that’s a significant fever and needs attention.

speaker: doctor
text: I’m continuing Amlodipine 5mg once daily for blood pressure and coronary protection.

speaker: patient
text: My legs have felt heavy, and I’m a bit lightheaded when standing up.

speaker: doctor
text: Your diastolic blood pressure is 72 mmHg—on the lower side, especially with that lightheadedness.

speaker: patient
text: I took my BP at home this morning before coming in—I think it was higher then.

speaker: doctor
text: Let me recheck—yes, now it’s 84 mmHg diastolic. That’s more consistent with your usual readings.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve also noticed my blood sugar has been spiking—especially at night.

speaker: doctor
text: Given your recent glucose trends and this new symptom burden, I’m going to switch you to Insulin glargine 10 units nightly to improve glycemic control.

speaker: patient
text: Okay, I’ll start that tonight. Should I still take my metformin?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes—continue metformin unless you develop GI upset. We’ll monitor closely.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been tired all week—even after sleeping. And I’ve had some shortness of breath climbing stairs.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s concerning. Let’s repeat the hemoglobin—we just got a result earlier, but given your fatigue and SOB, I want to verify.

speaker: doctor
text: The repeat shows 109 g/L—definitely lower than the 135 we saw earlier. We’ll investigate possible causes like inflammation or occult bleeding.

speaker: patient
text: That fever’s still there—same as when you measured it earlier: 39.2°C.
